english: idea, notion, conception, c. genit., odi tams-cád ráñ-gi séms-kyi snán-ba yin ('di thams cad rang gi sems kyi snang ba yin) all these things are only conceptions of your mind, your fancies Thgr.; skyid-sdúg-gi snan-ba (skyid sdug gi snang ba) śar (shar) Thgr.; okyágs-pai snán-ba byuñ ('khyags pa'i snang ba byung) Mil.; bkres-snáñ ye-méd-par gyúr-to (bkres snang ye med par gyur to) he was even without a thought of hunger Mil.; absolutely: *kyód-di náñ-wa gá-ru tañ soñ* (*khyod di nang wa ga ru tang song*) W. where are your thoughts wandering? ćós-la snán-ba sgyur (chos la snang ba sgyur) turn your mind to religion! Mil.; snáñ - ba ogyúr - ba (snang ba 'gyur ba) (τὸ μετανοεῖν) change of heart, conversion (not to be confounded with snáñ-bar ogyúr-ba (snang bar 'gyur ba) v. above). snán-ba bdé-ba (snang ba bde ba) pleased, cheerful, happy Pth.; in some expressions it is equivalent to soul. Most of the significations mentioned sub 3 seem not to have been in use in the older language. — okrul-snáñ ('khrul snang), oprul snáñ ('phrul snang) illusion, deception of the senses, deceit, error Mil., Glr., col. — ynyis-snáñ (gnyis snang) the arising of two ideas in the mind, ynyis-snáñ-gi rtóg - pa (gnyis snang gi rtog pa) hesitation, irresolution, wavering Mil. — mtoñ-snáñ (mthong snang) 1. the act of seeing, the sight, mtoñ-snáñ-gi sprúl-pa (mthong snang gi sprul pa) phantom, apparition, *toñ-náñ dé-mo* (*thong nang de mo*) W. a sight beautiful to look at, *toñ-náñ sóg-po* (*thong nang sog po*) of ugly appearance. 2. Cs.: manner or mode of viewing, point of view; yzigs-snáñ (gzigs snang) id. resp.; Pth.: yzigs - snáñ - la (gzigs snang la) according to his (supernatural) intuition (with reference to a holy person). — tsor-snáñ (tshor snang) the hearing, *tsor-náñ-la nyán-po* (*tshor nang la nyan po*) W. delightful to hear, pleasing to the ear. — bar-snáñ (bar snang) v. bar (bar). — rañ - snáñ (rang snang) one's own thoughts, ideas Mil.; the own mind Glr.; rañ-snáñ okrúl-pa (rang snang 'khrul pa) an illusion of fancy Thgr.; snáñ-grágs (snang grags) things seen and heard Mil. — snáñ-stoñ (snang stong) Mil. frq., prob. not 'empty show, delusive appearance' Sch., but: things (really) appearing and (yet) void, one of those frq. instances, where two words of opposite meaning are placed together, dbyer - méd (dbyer med) often being added, as a tertium quid (cf. Köpp. I, 598). — snáñ - dág (snang dag) (nañ - rtágs (nang rtags), brtág (brtag)?? Ld. nañ-stag (nang stag)) col. the inward man, the heart, the soul, *nañ-dág-la sám-pa śar soñ* (*nang dag la sam pa shar song*) W. a thought has risen in my soul; *nañ - dág čad soñ* (*nang dag chad song*) now he has felt it in his inmost soul, this will have struck home to his heart W.; *ñá-la nañ-dhág ma jhuñ* (*nga la nang dhag ma jhung*) C. I have not heard it, perceived it, minded it; *nañ-dhág ma jhě* (*nang dhag ma jhe*) C., *ma čẹ* (*ma ce*) W., I was not heedful, I made a mistake! — snán-ba-mta-yás (snang ba mtha' yas) = od-dpag-méd ('od dpag med) Amitabha, the fourth Dhyāni Buddha. — snáñ-me (snang me) v. nañ - mē (nang me). — snáñ-tsád (snang tshad) v. above I., 2. — snañ-tsúl (snang tshul) 1. the outward appearance, of a landscape = scenery Mil.; 2. appearance opp. to essence, ynas-tsul (gnas tshul) Was. (297). — snañ - mdzád (snang mdzad) v. rnáñ - par (rnang par). — snañ - śás (snang shas) thoughts, fancies (?) — snañ-srid (snang srid) (Ssk. संसार) the visible, external world frq. — snañ-ysál (snang gsal) shining brightly, brilliant; ćós-kyi snañ-ysál sgrón-me (chos kyi snang gsal sgron me) the bright light of doctrine Pth. — snañ-ñor ral odrum (snang nyor ral 'drum) Tar. 16 (?).

pf. bsnad (bsnad), imp. snod (snod), to hurt, to harm, to injure, c. accus., lus snád-nas (lus snad nas) being hurt in the body Dzl.; ñai rta snad gro (nga'i rta snad gro) or ooñ ('ong) my horse might be hurt Mil.; snád-kyis dógs - te (snad kyis dogs te) afraid of hurting him Dzl.; of horned cattle: to butt Sch.

resp. śañs (shangs), mucus, snivel, snot, snabs pyí - ba (snabs phyi ba) to blow one's nose, snabs-pyis (snabs phyis) pocket-handkerchief; snabs-lúg (snabs lug) snotty nose, snotty fellow Sch.; snabs-lúd (snabs lud), prob. also dar-snábs (dar snabs) Dom. = snabs (snabs); bé-snabs (be snabs) thick phlegm Cs.; snám-pa (snam pa) v. snom (snom).

woolen cloth; the common sort is not dyed, very coarse, and loosely woven; snám-bu spú-ċan (snam bu spu can) hairy cloth, napped cloth; snam-prúg (snam phrug), dbus-snám (dbus snam) Mil., fine cloth; go-snám (go snam) Cs. id.; snam-sbyár (snam sbyar) Lex. a sort of loose mantle for priests Cs. — *nám-ya* (*nam ya*) W. trowsers. — snam-yúg (snam yug), yúg-snam (yug snam) a whole piece or roll of woolen cloth. snam rás (snam ras) woolen cloth and cotton cloth Mil.
(Ü: *üm-bág* (*üm bag*)) bosom, snam-lógs (snam logs), snam-yzógs (snam gzogs) resp. side.
termin. of sna (sna); snar-bkáb (snar bkab) Wdk. fol. 464 nose-band(?) pocket-handkerchief(?); snár - kyu (snar kyu) guide-rope for camels, passing through their nose.
n. of a monastery, Köpp. II. 256; n. of a philologist Gram.
Cs. 1. of a white or light red colour (cf. skya-nár (skya nar)). - 2. long, oblong; cf. nár-mo (nar mo).
